extracted_information: This article from Replit provides a detailed comparison of three prominent web-based code editors: Ace, Monaco, and CodeMirror (specifically v6), based on Replit's six years of production experience. It covers their history, technical characteristics, performance, and real-world usage insights, including reasons for Replit's adoption and eventual transitions between them. The comparison is structured across several key criteria, with scores and qualitative descriptions.

stability.description: Extremely stable and reliable, battle-tested for over 10 years, no breaking changes, minor bugs patched quickly.

modularity_bundling_footprint.description: Slim, modular, supports lazy loading. Uses a homebrewed module system, requires some configuration to integrate.

performance.description: Very performant due to being built in an era of less powerful browsers and machines.

technical_details.maintenance: Actively maintained, but previously seen deprioritization after Cloud9 acquisition (by Amazon), leading to slow updates and issues.
technical_details.api: Feels a little dated but solid, easy to read codebase and monkey-patching due to stable internals.

Ace.mobile_support: Not-bad support, but does not come close to CodeMirror 6.

stability.description: Stable editing experience, bugs patched quickly (as it affects VSCode), continuous releases. API is not the most stable with subtle changes, no v1.0.0 semver.

modularity_bundling_footprint.description: Huge bundle size (~5 MB uncompressed), no lazy-loading possible. Requires special and difficult configurations in build systems (e.g., Webpack DLL, Webpack plugin).

performance.description: Has many performance optimizations but can be clunky, especially for users on low-powered machines.

technical_details.relationship: Powers VSCode; VSCode built around Monaco.
technical_details.build_tooling_integration: Did not mesh well with web ecosystem, required extensive Webpack configuration, increased build overhead. Later Webpack plugin improved but still problematic with Next.js.
technical_details.api: Good documentation, ships with TypeScript definitions. Extension points are somewhat limited and specific. Hard to monkey-patch due to complex and constantly changing internals. Replit stopped upgrading due to difficulty supporting custom features.

Monaco.mobile_support: Unusable on mobile. Codebase difficult to navigate, intertwined with VSCode, package feels like an afterthought. Replit attempted and abandoned a semi-usable fork.

stability.description: Still in beta, has subtle bugs but creator (Marijn) is extremely quick to respond and patch them. API is considered stable by creator. Increasingly adopted in production (e.g., Chrome DevTools considering migration).

modularity_bundling_footprint.description: Built with modern technologies, supports ES6 modules (no bundler needed). Lazy-loading features is easy using dynamic ES6 imports. Very modular with a slim core.

performance.description: Feels very performant; creator put much care into performance.

technical_details.design_philosophy: Rewrite (version 6) focused on modernizing and adding touch device support. Leans on native browser text editing (contentEditable). API design inspired by ProseMirror: tiny core, everything else is a plugin, modular, pluggable, functional.
technical_details.extensibility: Primary design principle. Core (`@codemirror/view` and `@codemirror/state`) is an extensible text area. All 'code' features (syntax highlighting, line numbers) implemented as extensions/packages. Generic extension points allow for powerful custom extensions.
technical_details.community_contribution_plans: Replit plans to open-source: Vim Mode, Emacs Mode, LSP client, Indentation Markers, Color Picker for CSS, language parsers.

CodeMirror 6.mobile_support: Objectively better than any other editor on mobile. Suitable even for native applications as a webview component, with serializable components for native code interop. Replit's mobile users showed 70% higher retention after CodeMirror rollout.

Ace.description: Excellent, with support for many features and languages, including basic JavaScript linting (JSHint) and autocomplete. UI is a little dated.

Monaco.description: Very polished UI. Ships with many features, including very good IntelliSense for HTML, CSS, and JavaScript out of the box.

CodeMirror 6.description: Requires some configuration to reach optimal state due to modularity. `basic-setup` package combines core modules. Basic UI is good.

Ace.description: Many configuration options and good extension points, though not very generic. APIs feel dated but solid. Easy to monkey-patch due to stable and readable codebase.

Monaco.description: Many configuration options and APIs to modify editor/features. Extension points are somewhat limited and specific. Difficult to monkey-patch due to complex/changing internals; Replit stopped upgrading due to this.

CodeMirror 6.description: Built with extensibility as a primary design principle. The core is an extensible text area; all features are extensions. Extensions act as great resources for authoring new ones. Generic extension points offer immense power.

Ace.description: Rich ecosystem, many articles/blogs. Supports many languages and community packages. API documentation is not the best but sufficient. Well-structured (albeit old) codebase provides supplementary resource. Good guide on website.

Monaco.description: Gathered momentum around 2018, then community energy dissipated. Some community-maintained packages on NPM. API documentation is good enough but could be better. No official guides, difficult to use codebase as supplementary resource.

CodeMirror 6.description: Strong community energy, Replit actively seeding with essential packages. Documentation is fantastic and improving, with excellent write-ups, getting started guides, and examples. Extensions serve as 'blessed' examples for development.

temporal_info.replit_codemirror_mobile_integration: Earlier this year (from article's publish date)
